analysis:
The strongest solutions share a clear structure: fast candidate pruning (remove dominated or reference‑worse points), build a single shared approximation of the contribution space (Monte‑Carlo samples), precompute compact domination masks in memory‑safe blocks, then do a lazy‑greedy selection (max‑heap) with incremental overlap subtraction. They follow with a bounded shortlist and a tiny exact‑HV polish (pygmo) only when affordable. These designs avoid repeated heavy hypervolume calls and reuse precomputed boolean data. 

Weaker variants recompute exact HVs in hot loops (per‑candidate or per‑swap), rebuild hypervolume objects repeatedly, run unconstrained evolutionary search or global local searches, and lack pruning or mask reuse; this causes large CPU and memory overheads. Avoidable inefficiencies observed: try/except and object construction inside inner loops, missing deterministic seeds, unnecessarily large/unbounded sample counts, not block‑processing boolean matrices, and poor tie‑breaking. Good heuristics balance MC accuracy (sample count scaling with k and D) against time, use vectorized/batched operations, and limit exact computations to a small shortlist.
experience: Prune dominated/ref‑worse points, use one shared MC sample set computed in memory‑safe blocks, precompute boolean masks, run lazy greedy via a max‑heap with incremental overlap updates, tie‑break by cheap box‑volumes, and do a bounded exact polish on a small shortlist. Cap samples/iterations, vectorize inner work, reuse hypervolume objects, and seed RNGs for reproducibility.
current_reflection: Prune dominated/ref‑worse points, use one shared MC sample set computed in memory‑safe blocks, precompute boolean masks, run lazy greedy via a max‑heap with incremental overlap updates, tie‑break by cheap box‑volumes, and do a bounded exact polish on a small shortlist. Cap samples/iterations, vectorize inner work, reuse hypervolume objects, and seed RNGs for reproducibility.
bad_reflections: Prune dominated points, reuse one shared MC sample set built in blocks, precompute boolean domination masks, select with a lazy greedy or incremental-count strategy, then do an exact‑HV polish on a tiny shortlist. Avoid exact HV inside inner loops and recreate heavy objects; vectorize mask ops and cap sample size proportional to k and D.

Favor: nondominated pruning + one shared MC sample set computed in memory‑safe blocks, lazy greedy selection (heap) with incremental overlap subtraction, and a tiny exact‑HV swap on a short shortlist. Cap memory, limit swap budget, reuse vectors/masks, and avoid repeated exact HV calls inside inner loops.

Prune dominated points early, reuse one MC sample set and block‑computed boolean masks, and drive selection with a lazy max‑heap of marginal gains (incrementally subtract overlaps). Limit exact hypervolume calls to a small shortlisted set and cap polish iterations. Vectorize updates, avoid per‑candidate full HV recomputations, and use box‑volume tie‑breakers and deterministic seeds for stable, fast heuristics.
comprehensive_reflection:
- Keywords: nondominated pruning, shared Monte Carlo samples, boolean masks, lazy greedy (max‑heap), incremental overlap, box‑volume tie‑break, bounded exact HV polish, vectorization, memory‑safe blocks, RNG seeding
- Advice: Prune dominated points, build one shared MC sample set in memory‑safe blocks and precompute masks; run lazy greedy via a max‑heap with incremental overlap updates, tie‑break by box volumes, then do a bounded exact HV polish on a small shortlist; vectorize and reuse heavy objects, cap samples/iterations.
- Avoid: Exact hypervolume inside inner loops, recreating heavy objects per candidate, per‑candidate full HV recomputations, unbounded sample/iteration budgets, and nondeterministic RNGs.
- Explanation: Keeps inner loops cheap and memory‑safe, makes results reproducible, and concentrates costly exact HV work on a tiny, high‑value shortlist.
